text:
The medals flowed on Day 11 of theParis2024OlympicsasTeam GBfollowedKeely Hodgkinson’s leadwith yet more success.

On four wheels, 16-year-old sensation Sky Brown brought home a second bronze in the women’s skateboarding, the same medal she won on her Olympic debut in Tokyo three years ago.

Ben Maher looked to defend his Olympic title in the individual jumping on his horse, Point Break, but it wasn’t to be for the 41-year-old, who missed out on the medals altogether.

Andrea Spendolini-Sirieix qualified in third for the 10m diving final, though wasunable to clinch another medalfollowing herbronze in the synchro.

In the velodrome British trio of Ed Lowe, Hamish Turnbull and Jack Carlin had to settle for the runners-up prize in the men’s team sprint, with the Netherlands setting two world records to clinch gold.

And it was silver again for Team GB later in the evening – this time on the track – as Josh Kerr was pipped to gold by America’s Cole Hocker after a pulsating men’s 1,500m race.

In the ring, GB’s Lewis Richardson had to settle for bronzeafter a controversial split-decision defeat,while Algeria’s Imane Khelif booked her place in the women’s 66kg final.

This blog is now closed. For the latest updates, visitMetro’s Sport page.

That’s all for today at the Paris Olympics and it’s been another enthralling day of action. We’ll be back at the same time tomorrow morning to keep you up to date with all the action.

Goodnight!

The action keeps coming tomorrow, with more medal hopes for Team GB on the track, in the velederome and elsewhere.

Here’s just some of what we’re set up for – make sure to set your alarms!

Athletics

Basketball

Beach volleyball

Boxing

Cycling (track)

Diving

Golf

Hockey

Sailing

Skateboarding

Sport climbing

Weightlifting

Here is how the medal table looks as Day 11 in Paris draws to a close. A trio of late gold medals move the USA ahead of China and into first place, while GB move closer to France having picked up two silvers and two bronzes.

It’s looking tight in that battle for third place.

Team GBboxer Lewis Richardson admitted he was left ‘devasted’ by his controversial semi-final defeat to Marco Verde in the ParisOlympic Games.

Here what the welterweight boxer had to say after he was forced to settle for a bronze medal in Paris:

Team GB star Lina Nielsen broke down in tears and opened up on her battle with multiple sclerosis after she fell in the semi-finals of the 400m women’s hurdles at theParisOlympics.

Nielsen had ambitions of making the final but the 28-year-old’s dreams went up in smoke as she clattered into the final barrier of the opening semi-final.

And after the race, the Brit was consoled by a reporter as she opened up on her difficult journey just to make it to Paris:

Kellie Harrington is overcome with emotion as the Irish fighter retains her 60kg Olympic title with a convincing display over old rival Yang Wenlu.

With that win – her last amateur bout – the 34-year-old becomes the first-ever Irish boxer to win gold in two consecutive Olympic Games and she celebrates wildly with her team and the hundreds of Irish fans crammed into the stadium here in Paris.

A great moment and the impressive summer Games continue for Ireland.

A great win against the reigning World champions as Brazil beat Spain 4-2 to book their place in the final of the women’s football.

They will take on USA, now managed by former Chelsea manager Emma Hayes, in Saturday’s final.

And it’s a dominant display from the number five seed Khelif who books her spot in the final with a unanimous decision win over Janjaem Suwannapheng.

The Algerian won both of the opening rounds by unanimous decision and made no mistake in the final three minutes, even forcing a standing count after a great combination.

It’s a great atmosphere at Court Philippe Chatrier as the Parisian crowd cheers on the victorious Khelif who will now go for gold later in the week as she bids to win her nation’s first boxing gold.

The Algerian boxer has endured a controversial road to this point but is now three rounds away from the gold medal bout in the women’s 66kg division.

Thai fighter Janjaem Suwannapheng is standing in her way.

An impressive display from GB’s Jacob Fincham-Dukes who comes up just short of the medals with a leap of 8.14m.

But it’s gold for Greece Miltiadis Tentoglou who defends his Olympic title with a mark of 8.48. Jamaica’s Wayne Pinnock came second with 8.36m and Italy’s Mattia Furlani took bronze with 8.34m.

GB’s Lewis Richardson thinks he has done enough but the welterweight comes up just short in his semi-final bout against Mexico’s Marco Verde.

A split-decision defeat means he has to settle for bronze and that will be Britain’s only medal in the ring at Paris this Games.

Verde, meanwhile, will now fight Uzbekistan’s Asadkhuja Muydinkhujaev for the gold medal.

It was a pulsating men’s 1,500m final but GB’s Josh Kerr was forced to settle for silver as America’s Cole Hocker claimed a shock win in Paris.

Former Olympic Champion Michael Johnson provided his analysis of the race:

American’s Gabby Thomas was the favourite and she made no mistake, pipping 100m winner Julien Alfred to take home the gold.

Brittany Brown takes bronze but it’s heartache for Team GB with Dina Asher-Smith and Daryll Neita coming fourth and fifth respectively.

And there’s plenty of interest from a Team GB perspective, with Dina Asher-Smith and Daryll Neita both involved.

Julien Alfred will look to do the double having won 100m gold earlier in the Games.

An Olympic record for Bahrain’s Yavi as she pips defending Olympic champion Peruth Chemutai on the line. Bronze for Kenya’s Faith Cherotich.

It’s a great run from GB’s Elizabeth Bird, too, who crosses the line in seventh but sets a new national record in the process.

‘I’m so proud of myself, I executed the best 1500m race of my life on the biggest stage by over a second,’ the Brit says speaking on Eurosport.

‘It’s difficult to control other people but I positioned myself to win and I did that. It wasn’t enough over the last 30m but I can walk away with my head held high.

‘All I can do is be focusing on my race. If you start worrying about the race and other peoples’ work you run a fine line of being disappointed everywhere.

‘I wanted gold, I got silver, it’s better than bronze – we’re moving in the right direction.’

A pulsating race all the way up to the finish line…

WOW!! ð¤¯USA’s Cole Hocker just shredded the script! He’s taken gold with GB’s Josh Kerr in silver. Jakob Ingebrigtsen dropped to fourth!#BBCOlympics#Olympics#Paris2024pic.twitter.com/TgAigLmcD0

Sebastian Coe billed tonight’s 1500m final as a ‘race for the ages’ between arch-rivals Josh Kerr and Jakob Ingebrigtsen – and it did not disappoint.

But there’s a huge shock in Paris as Cole Hocker wins gold for the USA!

Ingebrigtsen stormed to the front in a rapid start to the race and the only question was whether or not the Norwegian would be able to sustain the pace. The answer: no he couldn’t.

In a pulsating finish on the home straight, Kerr overtook Ingebrigtsen but was passed himself by Hocker, who held firm to secure a famous victory.

In the end, Ingebrigtsen crossed the finish line in fourth, with USA’s Yared Nuguse clinching bronze at the death.

What. A. Race.

Team GB take silver after a historic men’s team sprint final!

Ed Lowe, Hamish Turnbull and Jack Carlin knew they were in for an almighty battle against the Netherlands and it wasn’t to be for the British trio, who had to settle for the runners-up prize.

The Dutch outfit opened up a sizeable gap with one lap remaining of the velodrome and stormed home in 40.949 seconds to set a second world record in as many rides.

Different class from the Dutch.

Matthew Hudson-Smith is into the men’s 400m final â#BBCOlympics#Paris2024#Olympicspic.twitter.com/1JJG3rvm9e

It doesn’t get much more emphatic than this…

Team GB’s Matthew Hudson-Smith books his place in the men’s 400m final with a thunderous time of 44.07 seconds in his semi-final.

European silver medallist Charlie Dobson finished fifth in his heat and will not be competing in tomorrow’s showpiece at the Stade de France.

title: Olympics schedule and results: Sky Brown claims stunning bronze for Team GB | Metro News
description: Latest news and live updates from 2024 Paris Olympic Games on day 11's schedule: Athletics, track cycling, diving, skateboarding, boxing.
